heading: Prohibition on use of lead pipes, solder, and flux
label: 300g–6 Prohibition on use of lead pipes, solder, and flux

L. 102–54, § 13(q)(2)105 Stat. 279June 19, 1986June 13, 1991“(1).—ProhibitionThe Secretary of Housing and Urban Development and the Secretary of Veterans Affairs may not insure or guarantee a mortgage or furnish assistance with respect to newly constructed residential property which contains a potable water system unless such system uses only lead free pipe, solder, and flux.“(2).—Definition of lead freeFor purposes of paragraph (1) the term ‘lead free’—“(A)when used with respect to solders and flux refers to solders and flux containing not more than 0.2 percent lead, and“(B)when used with respect to pipes and pipe fittings refers to pipes and pipe fittings containing not more than 8.0 percent lead.“(3).—Effective dateParagraph (1) shall become effective 24 months after the enactment of this Act [].”June 19, 1986

children.label: i
children.text: any public water system; or

children.label: ii
children.text: any plumbing in a residential or nonresidential facility providing water for human consumption,

children.heading: Leaded joints
children.label: B Leaded joints
children.text: Subparagraph (A) shall not apply to leaded joints necessary for the repair of cast iron pipes.

children.label: i
children.text: The lead content in the construction materials of the public water distribution system.

children.label: ii
children.text: Corrosivity of the water supply sufficient to cause leaching of lead.

children.label: i
children.text: the potential sources of lead in the drinking water,

children.label: ii
children.text: potential adverse health effects,

children.label: iii
children.text: reasonably available methods of mitigating known or potential lead content in drinking water,

children.label: iv
children.text: any steps the system is taking to mitigate lead content in drinking water, and

children.label: v
children.text: the necessity for seeking alternative water supplies, if any.

children.label: A
children.text: for any person to introduce into commerce any pipe, or any pipe or plumbing fitting or fixture, that is not lead free, except for a pipe that is used in manufacturing or industrial processing;

children.label: B
children.text: for any person engaged in the business of selling plumbing supplies, except manufacturers, to sell solder or flux that is not lead free; or

children.label: C
children.text: for any person to introduce into commerce any solder or flux that is not lead free unless the solder or flux bears a prominent label stating that it is illegal to use the solder or flux in the installation or repair of any plumbing providing water for human consumption.

children.label: A
children.text: pipes, pipe fittings, plumbing fittings, or fixtures, including backflow preventers, that are used exclusively for nonpotable services such as manufacturing, industrial processing, irrigation, outdoor watering, or any other uses where the water is not anticipated to be used for human consumption; or

children.label: B
children.text: toilets, bidets, urinals, fill valves, flushometer valves, tub fillers, shower valves, fire hydrants, service saddles, or water distribution main gate valves that are 2 inches in diameter or larger.

children.heading: Enforcement of prohibition
children.label: 1 Enforcement of prohibition
children.text: The requirements of subsection (a)(1) shall be enforced in all States effective 24 months after . States shall enforce such requirements through State or local plumbing codes, or such other means of enforcement as the State may determine to be appropriate.June 19, 1986

children.heading: Enforcement of public notice requirements
children.label: 2 Enforcement of public notice requirements
children.text: The requirements of subsection (a)(2) shall apply in all States effective 24 months after .June 19, 1986

children.heading: Penalties
children.label: c Penalties
children.text: If the Administrator determines that a State is not enforcing the requirements of subsection (a) as required pursuant to subsection (b), the Administrator may withhold up to 5 percent of Federal funds available to that State for State program grants under .section 300j–2(a) of this title

children.label: A
children.text: not containing more than 0.2 percent lead when used with respect to solder and flux; and

children.label: B
children.text: not more than a weighted average of 0.25 percent lead when used with respect to the wetted surfaces of pipes, pipe fittings, plumbing fittings, and fixtures.

children.heading: Calculation
children.label: 2 Calculation
children.text: The weighted average lead content of a pipe, pipe fitting, plumbing fitting, or fixture shall be calculated by using the following formula: For each wetted component, the percentage of lead in the component shall be multiplied by the ratio of the wetted surface area of that component to the total wetted surface area of the entire product to arrive at the weighted percentage of lead of the component. The weighted percentage of lead of each wetted component shall be added together, and the sum of these weighted percentages shall constitute the weighted average lead content of the product. The lead content of the material used to produce wetted components shall be used to determine compliance with paragraph (1)(B). For lead content of materials that are provided as a range, the maximum content of the range shall be used.

children.heading: In general
children.label: 1 In general
children.text: The Administrator shall provide accurate and timely technical information and assistance to qualified third-party certifiers in the development of voluntary standards and testing protocols for the leaching of lead from new plumbing fittings and fixtures that are intended by the manufacturer to dispense water for human ingestion.

children.heading: In general
children.label: A In general
children.text: If a voluntary standard for the leaching of lead is not established by the date that is 1 year after , the Administrator shall, not later than 2 years after , promulgate regulations setting a health-effects-based performance standard establishing maximum leaching levels from new plumbing fittings and fixtures that are intended by the manufacturer to dispense water for human ingestion. The standard shall become effective on the date that is 5 years after the date of promulgation of the standard.August 6, 1996August 6, 1996

children.heading: Alternative requirement
children.label: B Alternative requirement
children.text: If regulations are required to be promulgated under subparagraph (A) and have not been promulgated by the date that is 5 years after , no person may import, manufacture, process, or distribute in commerce a new plumbing fitting or fixture, intended by the manufacturer to dispense water for human ingestion, that contains more than 4 percent lead by dry weight.August 6, 1996

children.label: A
children.text: risks associated with lead in drinking water;

children.label: B
children.text: the conditions that contribute to drinking water containing lead in a residence;

children.label: C
children.text: steps that States, public water systems, and consumers can take to reduce the risks of lead in drinking water; and

children.label: D
children.text: the availability of additional resources that consumers can use to minimize lead exposure, including information on sampling for lead in drinking water.

children.heading: Vulnerable populations
children.label: 2 Vulnerable populations
children.text: In making information available to the public under this subsection, the Administrator shall, subject to the availability of appropriations, carry out targeted outreach strategies that focus on educating groups within the general population that may be at greater risk than the general population of adverse health effects from exposure to lead in drinking water.
